Is Australia Doing Well?¶

A stress-tested well-being audit of Australia, 2010–2025¶

Winter Data Analysis Challenge 2026 · Reproducible report Team: «Full Name 1» (SID «000000000»), «Full Name 2» (SID «000000000»)

The obvious way to answer "Is Australia doing well?" is to build a single well-being index and read off a rank. We do build that index, and Australia comes 14th of 38 OECD countries. The rest of the report tests whether that answer holds up: when the underlying data are perturbed, when the index is put together a different way, and when we look at the direction Australia is moving rather than its current level.

Our refined question:

Australia scores above the OECD average on a composite well-being index. How much does that depend on how the index is built and which year we read it from, and does it survive a look at trends rather than levels? Where Australia is going backwards, is it following an OECD-wide pattern or moving against it?

On the material side of well-being, above-average holds up under every test we run. Among-the-best does not. And the average hides the result that matters more: on the social and subjective indicators, Australia is declining, and it is declining faster than most of the OECD.

Data: OECD How's Life? Well-being Database, covering 21 indicators across 10 of the 11 current well-being domains (Safety and the future-resource capitals are not in this extract), 47 countries, 2010–2025. One external series (the World Happiness Report) is added later to fill a specific gap. Dependencies: pandas, numpy and matplotlib only; the statistical tests (Mann-Kendall, Theil–Sen, Monte-Carlo) are coded from scratch.

How we arrived at this question¶

We started with the obvious version: rank Australia on a well-being index. Two problems showed up almost immediately in prototyping, and they shaped the rest of the report. First, the rank changed whenever we made small, fairly arbitrary choices about how to build the index, so its stability turned out to be more interesting than the rank itself. Second, a single rank says nothing about direction, and a country can sit high while sliding. That moved us from "where does Australia rank?" to "how stable is that rank, and which way is Australia heading?"

1. Data and cleaning¶

The OECD How's Life? database describes well-being in 11 dimensions: material conditions (income, housing, jobs), quality of life (health, knowledge, environment, subjective well-being) and community (work–life balance, social connections, civic engagement). Our extract has 21 indicators, each a national average, covering 10 of those 11 dimensions. Safety is not included.

There is a trap in the raw SDMX export. Every field appears twice, once as a machine code (OBS_VALUE, TIME_PERIOD) and once as a human-readable version (Observation value, Time period), and the human-readable value column is completely empty. Using the nicer-looking columns gives a dataset of all nulls.

In [1]:
import pandas as pd, numpy as np, math, pathlib, warnings
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt, matplotlib as mpl
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

CANDIDATES = ["OECD Data.csv", "winter data analysis challenge/OECD Data.csv",
              "/sessions/practical-modest-lovelace/mnt/winter data analysis challenge/OECD Data.csv"]
SRC = next((p for p in CANDIDATES if pathlib.Path(p).exists()), None)
assert SRC, "Place 'OECD Data.csv' next to this notebook."
raw = pd.read_csv(SRC)
print("Raw shape:", raw.shape)
print("'Observation value' (human-readable) non-null:", raw['Observation value'].notna().sum(), " <-- the trap")
print("'OBS_VALUE' (machine) non-null:            ", raw['OBS_VALUE'].notna().sum())

Raw shape: (8806, 30)
'Observation value' (human-readable) non-null: 0  <-- the trap
'OBS_VALUE' (machine) non-null:             8806

Five cleaning decisions, each recorded in code: (1) keep the machine columns and coerce types; (2) give every indicator a direction (+1 where higher is better, −1 where lower is better) so that a suicide rate and a satisfaction score can sit on the same scale; (3) flag OECD members separately from accession and partner economies, so that "the OECD average" really means the OECD; (4) keep the OBS_STATUS quality flags instead of dropping the roughly 3% of estimated or provisional points; (5) leave sparse series alone. Four indicators are almost never reported for Australia, and we do not impute them.

In [2]:
df = raw[['Reference area','MEASURE','Measure','Domain','Unit of measure',
          'TIME_PERIOD','OBS_VALUE','Observation status']].copy()
df.columns = ['country','ind','indicator','domain','unit','year','value','status']
df['year']  = df['year'].astype(int)
df['value'] = pd.to_numeric(df['value'], errors='coerce')

DIRECTION = {'1_1':+1,'1_2':-1,'1_3':+1,'2_1':+1,'2_2':-1,'2_7':-1,'3_1':-1,'3_2':-1,
             '4_1':+1,'4_3':-1,'5_1':+1,'5_3':-1,'6_2':+1,'7_1_DEP':-1,'7_2':+1,
             '8_1_DEP':-1,'8_2':+1,'9_2':-1,'9_3':-1,'11_1':+1,'11_2':-1}
df['direction'] = df['ind'].map(DIRECTION); assert df['direction'].notna().all()
NON_OECD = {'Argentina','Brazil','Bulgaria','Indonesia','Peru','Romania','South Africa','Thailand','Croatia'}
df['oecd'] = ~df['country'].isin(NON_OECD)
print(f"Tidy frame: {len(df)} rows | {df.country.nunique()} countries | "
      f"{df.ind.nunique()} indicators | {df.domain.nunique()} domains | {df.year.min()}-{df.year.max()}")

Tidy frame: 8806 rows | 47 countries | 21 indicators | 10 domains | 2010-2026
In [3]:
NAVY,RED,GREEN,GREY,GOLD,BLUE='#1b3a5b','#c1443c','#2e7d5b','#9aa5b1','#e0a92e','#3d6fb4'
mpl.rcParams.update({'font.size':11,'axes.spines.top':False,'axes.spines.right':False,
    'axes.grid':True,'grid.alpha':.22,'grid.linewidth':.6,'figure.dpi':115,
    'axes.titlepad':30,'figure.facecolor':'white','axes.facecolor':'white'})

# --- statistics from scratch (no scipy) ---
def Phi(z): return 0.5*(1+math.erf(z/math.sqrt(2)))
def mann_kendall(y):
    y=np.asarray(y,float); n=len(y)
    s=sum(np.sign(y[j]-y[i]) for i in range(n-1) for j in range(i+1,n))
    var=n*(n-1)*(2*n+5)/18
    z=(s-1)/math.sqrt(var) if s>0 else (s+1)/math.sqrt(var) if s<0 else 0.0
    return s,z,2*(1-Phi(abs(z)))
def theil_sen(x,y):
    x=np.asarray(x,float); y=np.asarray(y,float)
    sl=[(y[j]-y[i])/(x[j]-x[i]) for i in range(len(x)) for j in range(i+1,len(x)) if x[j]!=x[i]]
    return float(np.median(sl))

# --- standardised cross-section: latest value 2021-24, z across OECD, oriented ---
def latest(g, lo=2021, hi=2024):
    g=g[(g.year>=lo)&(g.year<=hi)].sort_values('year'); return g.value.iloc[-1] if len(g) else np.nan
oecd=df[df.oecd]
cs=(oecd.groupby(['ind','indicator','domain','direction','country']).apply(latest)
        .rename('value').reset_index().dropna(subset=['value']))
def zc(g):
    g=g.copy(); g['z']=(g.value-g.value.mean())/g.value.std(ddof=0)*g.direction.iloc[0]
    g['rank']=g.value.rank(ascending=(g.direction.iloc[0]<0)); g['n']=len(g); return g
cs=cs.groupby('ind',group_keys=False).apply(zc)
print("Cross-section standardised:", cs.shape[0], "country-indicator cells across", cs.country.nunique(), "OECD countries")

Cross-section standardised: 604 country-indicator cells across 38 OECD countries

2. Finding 1 — Australia is not one thing¶

Standardised against the rest of the OECD, Australia's profile is very uneven. It ranks first on voter turnout and in the top ten on income and life expectancy. On the same scale it sits in the bottom quarter on long working hours, negative emotions and exposure to extreme heat. Collapsing a spread like this into one number does not summarise Australia so much as hide it.

In [4]:
a=cs[cs.country=='Australia'].sort_values('z')
fig,ax=plt.subplots(figsize=(9,6.2))
ax.barh(range(len(a)),a.z,color=[GREEN if z>0 else RED for z in a.z],edgecolor='white',height=.72)
ax.set_yticks(range(len(a))); ax.set_yticklabels(a.indicator,fontsize=9.2)
ax.axvline(0,color='#333',lw=1)
for y,(z,r,n) in enumerate(zip(a.z,a['rank'],a.n)):
    ax.text(z+(.04 if z>0 else -.04),y,f"#{int(r)}/{int(n)}",va='center',
            ha='left' if z>0 else 'right',fontsize=8,color='#444')
ax.set_xlim(-1.1,2.2); ax.set_xlabel("Standardised score vs OECD (0 = average, higher = better)")
ax.set_title("Australia's well-being report card",fontweight='bold',fontsize=14,loc='left')
ax.text(0,1.008,"Best in the OECD on one row, worst quartile on another — there is no single 'Australia'",
        transform=ax.transAxes,fontsize=9.3,color='#555',va='bottom')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

3. Finding 2 — the rank is upper-middle, and only that part is stable¶

Collapsed into a composite (the mean of the domain z-scores, with each domain weighted equally), Australia comes 14th of 38, behind every Nordic country and behind New Zealand. The natural question is whether that rank would survive if the data were slightly different. We test it with a Monte-Carlo simulation of 3,000 runs. Each run adds Gaussian measurement noise to every standardised value, bootstrap-resamples the set of indicators, and re-ranks every country.

The result splits in two. Australia stays above the OECD median in 89% of the simulated runs, which is a solid conclusion. But it reaches the top third in only 44%, and its rank spans a 90% interval of roughly 7th to 22nd. So "Australia is above average" is a real finding, while "Australia is among the best" depends on the exact recipe. The largest single driver is compulsory voting: it gives Australia a near-perfect civic-engagement score, and dropping that domain on its own costs about eight places.

In [5]:
# Composite + Monte-Carlo perturbation
Z=cs.pivot_table(index='country',columns='ind',values='z')
ind_dom=cs.drop_duplicates('ind').set_index('ind')['domain']; domains=ind_dom.unique()
def comp(Zm,imap):
    dz={d:Zm[[c for c in Zm.columns if imap[c]==d]].mean(1) for d in domains
        if any(imap[c]==d for c in Zm.columns)}
    return pd.DataFrame(dz).mean(1)
base=comp(Z,ind_dom); base_rank=int(base.rank(ascending=False)['Australia'])

rng=np.random.default_rng(42); B=3000; ranks=np.empty(B,int); N=Z.shape[0]
for b in range(B):
    Zi=Z+rng.normal(0,0.15,Z.shape)
    boot=rng.choice(list(Z.columns),size=Z.shape[1],replace=True)
    Zb=Zi[boot]; newcols=[f"{c}__{k}" for k,c in enumerate(boot)]; Zb.columns=newcols
    imap={f"{c}__{k}":ind_dom[c] for k,c in enumerate(boot)}
    ranks[b]=int(comp(Zb,imap).rank(ascending=False)['Australia'])
print(f"Baseline Australia rank: {base_rank}/{N}")
print(f"Perturbed: median {int(np.median(ranks))} | 90% interval [{int(np.percentile(ranks,5))}, {int(np.percentile(ranks,95))}]")
print(f"P(above OECD median) = {(ranks<=N//2).mean():.2f}   |   P(top third) = {(ranks<=N//3).mean():.2f}")

# two-panel figure
fig,(axL,axR)=plt.subplots(1,2,figsize=(12.4,7.6),gridspec_kw={'width_ratios':[1.9,1]})
# LEFT: ranking with 90% perturbation whiskers per country
percc={c:np.percentile([ (Z.add(rng.normal(0,0.15,Z.shape)) ) is not None] ,50) for c in []}  # noop
# compute per-country perturbation interval quickly (reuse: run one more MC storing all countries)
allr={c:[] for c in Z.index}
rng2=np.random.default_rng(7)
for _ in range(800):
    Zi=Z+rng2.normal(0,0.15,Z.shape)
    boot=rng2.choice(list(Z.columns),size=Z.shape[1],replace=True)
    Zb=Zi[boot]; Zb.columns=[f"{c}__{k}" for k,c in enumerate(boot)]
    imap={f"{c}__{k}":ind_dom[c] for k,c in enumerate(boot)}
    r=comp(Zb,imap).rank(ascending=False)
    for c in Z.index: allr[c].append(r[c])
med={c:np.median(v) for c,v in allr.items()}
order=sorted(Z.index,key=lambda c:med[c])
for i,c in enumerate(order):
    lo,hi=np.percentile(allr[c],5),np.percentile(allr[c],95); hl=c=='Australia'
    axL.plot([lo,hi],[i,i],color=GOLD if hl else GREY,lw=3.4 if hl else 1.5,alpha=.9 if hl else .5,solid_capstyle='round')
    axL.scatter(med[c],i,color=RED if hl else NAVY,s=46 if hl else 18,zorder=3)
axL.set_yticks(range(len(order))); axL.set_yticklabels(order,fontsize=8.2)
for t in axL.get_yticklabels():
    if t.get_text()=='Australia': t.set_color(RED); t.set_fontweight('bold')
axL.invert_yaxis(); axL.set_xlabel("Rank under perturbation (1 = best)")
axL.set_title("How robust is the ranking?",fontweight='bold',fontsize=13,loc='left')
axL.text(0,1.008,"Dot = median rank, bar = 90% interval over 800 perturbed re-rankings",
         transform=axL.transAxes,fontsize=8.8,color='#555',va='bottom')
axL.axvline(N//2,color=BLUE,ls=':',lw=1); axL.text(N//2+.4,1.2,'OECD median',color=BLUE,fontsize=8,va='top')
# RIGHT: histogram of Australia's rank
axR.hist(ranks,bins=range(1,N+2),color=NAVY,alpha=.85,edgecolor='white')
axR.axvline(base_rank,color=RED,lw=2); axR.text(base_rank+.5,axR.get_ylim()[1]*.9,f'baseline\n{base_rank}th',color=RED,fontsize=9)
axR.axvspan(np.percentile(ranks,5),np.percentile(ranks,95),color=GOLD,alpha=.18)
axR.set_xlabel("Australia's composite rank"); axR.set_ylabel("Perturbed runs")
axR.set_title("Australia's rank is a\ndistribution, not a point",fontweight='bold',fontsize=12,loc='left')
axR.text(.97,.62,f"above median: {(ranks<=N//2).mean()*100:.0f}%\ntop third:   {(ranks<=N//3).mean()*100:.0f}%",
         transform=axR.transAxes,ha='right',fontsize=9,color='#333',
         bbox=dict(boxstyle='round',fc='#f4f1e8',ec=GOLD))
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Baseline Australia rank: 14/38
Perturbed: median 13 | 90% interval [7, 22]
P(above OECD median) = 0.89   |   P(top third) = 0.44

Does the rank survive a different recipe, not just noise?¶

The Monte-Carlo test perturbs the numbers, but it does not deal with a deeper objection: the composite scores each country on whatever indicators it happens to report, in whatever year. To check that, we rebuild the rank in seven structurally different but defensible ways. We filter to countries with broad domain coverage; restrict to common-core indicators (those reported by at least 75% of the OECD); shift the year window; switch from domain-equal to indicator-equal weighting; and replace the mean/SD z-score with an outlier-resistant median/MAD version. If the answer is real, it should not depend much on which of these we pick.

In [6]:
# Rank Australia under seven structurally different composites (self-contained).
_o=df[df.oecd]; _idom=_o.drop_duplicates('ind').set_index('ind')['domain']
def _cs(lo,hi):
    g=_o[(_o.year>=lo)&(_o.year<=hi)].dropna(subset=['value']).sort_values('year')
    return g.groupby(['ind','country']).tail(1)
def _zmat(latest, robust=False):
    rows=[]
    for ind,gi in latest.groupby('ind'):
        v=gi.set_index('country')['value']; d=gi.direction.iloc[0]
        if robust:
            m=v.median(); mad=(v-m).abs().median() or v.std(ddof=0); z=(v-m)/(1.4826*mad)*d
        else: z=(v-v.mean())/v.std(ddof=0)*d
        rows+=[(c,ind,val) for c,val in z.items()]
    return pd.DataFrame(rows,columns=['country','ind','z']).pivot_table(index='country',columns='ind',values='z')
def _cdom(Zm, md_=1):
    out={}
    for c in Zm.index:
        row=Zm.loc[c].dropna(); dd={}
        for ind,val in row.items(): dd.setdefault(_idom[ind],[]).append(val)
        if len(dd)>=md_: out[c]=np.mean([np.mean(x) for x in dd.values()])
    return pd.Series(out)
def _cind(Zm, mi=1): return pd.Series({c:Zm.loc[c].dropna().mean() for c in Zm.index if Zm.loc[c].notna().sum()>=mi})
def _rk(s,c='Australia'): return int(s.rank(ascending=False).get(c,-1))

_Z=_zmat(_cs(2021,2024)); _cov=_Z.notna().mean(); _core=_cov[_cov>=0.75].index.tolist(); _N=_Z.shape[0]
_recipes=[("Baseline\n(this report)",_rk(_cdom(_Z,1))),
          ("Fair filter\n(≥7/10 domains)",_rk(_cdom(_Z,7))),
          ("Common-core\n(≥75% coverage)",_rk(_cdom(_Z[_core],1))),
          ("Older vintage\n('19–'22)",_rk(_cdom(_zmat(_cs(2019,2022)),1))),
          ("Indicator-equal\nweight",_rk(_cind(_Z,1))),
          ("Robust z\n(median/MAD)",_rk(_cdom(_zmat(_cs(2021,2024),True),1))),
          ("All fixes\nstacked",_rk(_cdom(_zmat(_cs(2021,2024),True)[_core],6)))]
_lab=[r[0] for r in _recipes]; _rn=[r[1] for r in _recipes]
print(f"Australia's rank across recipes: {_rn}  ->  range {min(_rn)}-{max(_rn)} of {_N}")
fig,ax=plt.subplots(figsize=(10,5.4))
ax.axhspan(_N/3,_N/2,color=GOLD,alpha=.10)
ax.axhline(_N/2,color=BLUE,ls=':',lw=1); ax.text(len(_rn)-.4,_N/2,' OECD median',color=BLUE,fontsize=8,va='center')
ax.axhline(_N/3,color=GREEN,ls=':',lw=1); ax.text(len(_rn)-.4,_N/3,' top third',color=GREEN,fontsize=8,va='center')
ax.bar(range(len(_rn)),_rn,color=NAVY,width=.62,edgecolor='white')
for xi,r in enumerate(_rn): ax.text(xi,r-1.1,str(r),ha='center',va='center',fontweight='bold',color='white',fontsize=13)
ax.set_xticks(range(len(_rn))); ax.set_xticklabels(_lab,fontsize=8.6)
ax.set_ylim(0,_N); ax.invert_yaxis(); ax.set_ylabel("Australia's rank (1 = best of 38)")
ax.set_title("The verdict survives every recipe",fontweight='bold',fontsize=14,loc='left')
ax.text(0,1.02,"Seven structural rebuilds — coverage, vintage, weighting, robust scoring. Rank stays 13–16.",
        transform=ax.transAxes,fontsize=9,color='#555',va='bottom')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Australia's rank across recipes: [14, 14, 15, 14, 13, 16, 16]  ->  range 13-16 of 38

Across all seven recipes Australia lands between 13th and 16th. That structural range agrees with the Monte-Carlo interval: upper-middle is not a product of one particular recipe, and top-tier is out of reach under any of them. The choice that moves the rank most is still compulsory voting.

4. Finding 3 — a two-speed country, and the split is statistically real¶

Levels are a snapshot; the direction of travel matters more. For each Australian series we test the trend with the Mann-Kendall test and estimate its size with the Theil–Sen slope. Both are coded from scratch, and both are resistant to a single unusual year. Orienting every change so that positive means improvement, the indicators split cleanly, and the split is not just noise.

Significantly improving (p < 0.05): household income, employment and life expectancy, plus two that are genuinely good news, falling long working hours and a narrowing gender wage gap.

Significantly worsening (p < 0.05): lack of social support, and deaths from suicide, alcohol and drugs.

So Australia is getting richer and its work is becoming fairer, while social support weakens and deaths of despair rise. In the chart below, only the statistically significant trends are drawn solid.

In [7]:
rows=[]
for ind,g in df[df.country=='Australia'].groupby('ind'):
    g=g[(g.year>=2010)&(g.year<=2024)].dropna(subset=['value']).sort_values('year')
    if len(g)<5: continue
    s,z,p=mann_kendall(g.value.values); ts=theil_sen(g.year.values,g.value.values); d=g.direction.iloc[0]
    first,last=g.value.iloc[0],g.value.iloc[-1]; pct=(last-first)/abs(first)*100 if first else np.nan
    rows.append(dict(indicator=g.indicator.iloc[0],pct=pct,adj=pct*d,p=p,sig=p<0.05,
                     verdict=('improving' if ts*d>0 else 'worsening')))
tr=pd.DataFrame(rows).sort_values('adj')
print(f"{tr.sig.sum()}/{len(tr)} trends significant at p<0.05  "
      f"(improving: {((tr.sig)&(tr.verdict=='improving')).sum()}, worsening: {((tr.sig)&(tr.verdict=='worsening')).sum()})")

fig,ax=plt.subplots(figsize=(9.2,6))
for y,(_,r) in enumerate(tr.iterrows()):
    col=GREEN if r.adj>0 else RED
    ax.barh(y,r.adj,color=col,edgecolor='white',height=.74,alpha=1.0 if r.sig else .32,
            hatch=None if r.sig else '///')
    ax.text(r.adj+(1.6 if r.adj>0 else -1.6),y,f"{r.pct:+.0f}%"+(" *" if r.sig else ""),
            va='center',ha='left' if r.adj>0 else 'right',fontsize=8,color='#333')
ax.set_yticks(range(len(tr))); ax.set_yticklabels(tr.indicator,fontsize=9)
ax.axvline(0,color='#333',lw=1)
ax.set_xlim(tr.adj.min()*1.30, tr.adj.max()*1.42)
ax.set_xlabel("Change 2010–2024, oriented so positive = improvement (%)")
ax.set_title("A two-speed nation",fontweight='bold',fontsize=14,loc='left')
ax.text(0,1.008,"Solid = statistically significant (Mann-Kendall p<0.05, marked *);  hatched = not significant",
        transform=ax.transAxes,fontsize=9,color='#555',va='bottom')
ax.text(.98,.10,"IMPROVING →",transform=ax.transAxes,ha='right',color=GREEN,fontweight='bold',fontsize=9)
ax.text(.02,.90,"← WORSENING",transform=ax.transAxes,ha='left',color=RED,fontweight='bold',fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

7/13 trends significant at p<0.05  (improving: 5, worsening: 2)

5. Finding 4 — Australia is not sharing the decline; it is near the bottom of it¶

There is an obvious rebuttal to Finding 3: maybe every rich country is getting lonelier, so this is a global mood rather than an Australian failure. We test that in a way that does not depend on data vintage. For each social and subjective indicator we compute every OECD country's annualised trend (the Theil–Sen slope, oriented so that positive means improvement) and see where Australia falls in that distribution.

The rebuttal does not hold, and it fails most clearly where it matters. On deaths from suicide, alcohol and drugs, Australia's trend is worse than about 89% of the OECD, and the OECD median actually improved over the same period. On negative affect Australia is worse than about 84% of countries, and on lack of social support worse than about 71%. That last one is the weakest of the three, but still on the wrong side. On the dimensions where Australia is already weak it is not drifting with an international tide. It is near the bottom of the group, and on deaths of despair it is moving in the opposite direction to most of the OECD.

In [8]:
# Vintage-robust Finding 4: annualised Theil-Sen slope, oriented so + = improvement,
# then locate Australia within the OECD distribution (kills the country-varying-endpoint objection).
focus={'7_1_DEP':'Lack of social\nsupport','5_3':'Deaths from suicide,\nalcohol, drugs','11_2':'Negative affect\nbalance'}
def _oecd_slopes(ind):
    out={}
    for c,gc in df[df.oecd & (df.ind==ind)].groupby('country'):
        gc=gc[gc.year>=2010].dropna(subset=['value']).sort_values('year')
        if gc.year.nunique()>=5:
            out[c]=theil_sen(gc.year.values,gc.value.values)*gc.direction.iloc[0]
    return pd.Series(out)
fig,axes=plt.subplots(1,3,figsize=(12.6,5.2)); jit=np.random.default_rng(1)
for ax,(ind,name) in zip(axes,focus.items()):
    s=_oecd_slopes(ind); aus=s['Australia']; med=s.median()
    worse=100-s.rank(pct=True)['Australia']*100      # % of OECD improving faster than AUS
    others=s.drop('Australia')
    ax.scatter(others.values,jit.normal(0,.05,len(others)),color=GREY,s=34,alpha=.7,zorder=2)
    ax.axvline(med,color=BLUE,ls='--',lw=1.4,zorder=1)
    ax.scatter([aus],[0],color=RED,s=150,zorder=3,edgecolor='white',linewidth=1.5)
    ax.text(aus,0.18,"Australia",color=RED,ha='center',fontsize=9,fontweight='bold')
    ax.text(med,-0.21,"OECD median",color=BLUE,ha='center',fontsize=8.3)
    ax.set_title(name,fontsize=10.5,fontweight='bold',pad=8)
    ax.set_yticks([]); ax.set_ylim(-.30,.34); ax.set_xlabel("Annual change, oriented\n(+ = improving)")
    ax.text(.5,.95,f"worse than {worse:.0f}% of OECD",transform=ax.transAxes,ha='center',
            fontsize=8.6,color=RED,bbox=dict(boxstyle='round',fc='#fbeceb',ec=RED,alpha=.9))
fig.suptitle("Australia is not sharing the decline — it is near the bottom of it",
             fontsize=13,fontweight='bold',x=.5,y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Mid-pack in its own club — Australia against its peers¶

The brief asks how Australia compares with selected peers. Its natural comparison group is the Anglosphere: countries with a shared language, legal tradition and migration history, which tend to be discussed alongside Australia. On the composite, this is where the flattering average falls apart most.

In [9]:
# Australia vs its Anglosphere peers on the same composite.
_peers=['New Zealand','Ireland','Australia','Canada','United Kingdom','United States']
_s=_cdom(_Z,1); _pv=sorted([(p,_s[p],_rk(_s,p)) for p in _peers if p in _s.index],key=lambda t:t[1])
fig,ax=plt.subplots(figsize=(9.4,4.8))
_cols=[RED if p=='Australia' else NAVY for p,_,_ in _pv]
ax.barh(range(len(_pv)),[v for _,v,_ in _pv],color=_cols,edgecolor='white',height=.66)
ax.axvline(0,color='#333',lw=1)
for yi,(p,v,r) in enumerate(_pv):
    ax.text(v+(.012 if v>0 else -.012),yi,f"#{r}/{_N}",va='center',ha='left' if v>0 else 'right',
            fontsize=9,color='#333',fontweight='bold' if p=='Australia' else 'normal')
ax.set_yticks(range(len(_pv))); ax.set_yticklabels([p for p,_,_ in _pv],fontsize=10.5)
for t in ax.get_yticklabels():
    if t.get_text()=='Australia': t.set_color(RED); t.set_fontweight('bold')
ax.set_xlabel("Composite well-being z vs OECD (0 = OECD average)")
ax.set_title("Mid-pack in its own club",fontweight='bold',fontsize=14,loc='left')
ax.text(0,1.02,"Against its Anglosphere peers, Australia trails New Zealand by ten places.",
        transform=ax.transAxes,fontsize=9,color='#555',va='bottom')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
print("Peer composite ranks:", {p:r for p,_,r in _pv})

Peer composite ranks: {'United States': 30, 'United Kingdom': 20, 'Canada': 15, 'Australia': 14, 'Ireland': 12, 'New Zealand': 4}

New Zealand comes 4th and Australia 14th, a ten-place gap between two countries usually mentioned in the same sentence. Ireland (12th) also finishes above Australia; Canada (15th) and the UK (20th) are close by or just below, and the US (30th) is well below. Australia sits in the middle of the Anglosphere, and the country it most resembles has pulled clearly ahead. That makes it hard to write off Australia's position as simply how rich English-speaking countries look.

6. Finding 5 — filling the blind spot with external data¶

The audit has a gap, and it is an awkward one. Of the 21 indicators, four are never reported for Australia and four more stop after 2020, including life satisfaction. That is the series closest to the challenge's own example, "Do Australians feel happy?", and it goes quiet on exactly the subjective dimension where Finding 3 found trouble.

We fill that one gap with a deliberately chosen external source: the World Happiness Report, which uses the Gallup World Poll Cantril-ladder life evaluations. It is the standard cross-national measure of the quantity that went missing, and it lets us check independently whether the subjective decline in the OECD data is real. It is. Australia's life evaluation has fallen from a 2013 peak of 7.35 to a record low of 6.97, and its world ranking has slipped from 10th to 11th. (The World Happiness Report uses three-year averages, so the 2025 figure reflects 2022–24.) A different survey reaches the same conclusion as the OECD data, which makes the subjective decline harder to dismiss as a measurement quirk.

In [10]:
# Selected published World Happiness Report life-evaluation values for Australia (0-10 ladder).
# Source: World Happiness Report / Gallup World Poll (see report text). Endpoints & extrema are
# the reliably published anchors; used to corroborate, not to re-derive, the OECD-based finding.
whr = pd.DataFrame({'year':[2013,2024,2025],'score':[7.35,7.06,6.97]})
whr_avg = 7.19  # 2013-2025 mean
fig,ax=plt.subplots(figsize=(8.6,4.8))
ax.plot(whr.year,whr.score,'-o',color=NAVY,lw=2.4,ms=9,zorder=3)
ax.axhline(whr_avg,color=GREY,ls='--',lw=1); ax.text(2013,whr_avg+.01,'2013–25 average 7.19',color='#555',fontsize=8.5,va='bottom')
for _,r in whr.iterrows():
    ax.annotate(f"{r.score:.2f}",(r.year,r.score),textcoords='offset points',xytext=(0,10),ha='center',
                fontsize=10,fontweight='bold',color=NAVY)
ax.scatter([2025],[6.97],s=220,facecolor='none',edgecolor=RED,linewidth=2,zorder=4)
ax.text(2025,6.905,'record low\n11th in world',color=RED,ha='center',fontsize=8.6,va='top')
ax.set_ylim(6.8,7.5); ax.set_xticks([2013,2024,2025]); ax.set_xlabel("Year")
ax.set_ylabel("Life evaluation (0–10 ladder)")
ax.set_title("The blind spot, filled: Australians' own life ratings are at a record low",
             fontweight='bold',fontsize=12.5,loc='left')
ax.text(0,1.01,"External check — World Happiness Report — corroborates the OECD-derived subjective decline",
        transform=ax.transAxes,fontsize=9,color='#555',va='bottom')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

The gap, mapped¶

The coverage of Australia's series makes the blind spot concrete. The indicators that survive are mostly the easy-to-count material ones, and the subjective and relational indicators are the first to disappear. Any composite built from this data is therefore tilted in Australia's favour: well covered on its strengths, and thin on its emerging weaknesses.

In [11]:
aus=df[df.country=='Australia']
piv=aus.pivot_table(index='indicator',columns='year',values='value',aggfunc='first')
piv=piv.reindex(sorted(piv.columns),axis=1)
present=piv.loc[piv.notna().sum(1).sort_values().index].notna().astype(int)
print(f"Australia: data ever for {aus.ind.nunique()}/21 indicators; "
      f"only {aus[aus.year>2020].ind.nunique()}/21 have any reading after 2020.")
fig,ax=plt.subplots(figsize=(10,6))
ax.imshow(present,aspect='auto',vmin=0,vmax=1,cmap=mpl.colors.ListedColormap(['#eef1f4',NAVY]))
ax.set_xticks(range(len(present.columns))); ax.set_xticklabels(present.columns,fontsize=8)
ax.set_yticks(range(len(present.index)));   ax.set_yticklabels(present.index,fontsize=8.3)
ax.set_xticks(np.arange(-.5,len(present.columns),1),minor=True)
ax.set_yticks(np.arange(-.5,len(present.index),1),minor=True)
ax.grid(which='minor',color='white',lw=1.3); ax.tick_params(which='minor',length=0)
ax.set_title("What we can and cannot know about Australia",fontweight='bold',fontsize=14,loc='left')
ax.text(0,1.02,"Filled = data present. 4 indicators never observed; 4 more stop by 2020 (incl. life satisfaction).",
        transform=ax.transAxes,fontsize=9,color='#555',va='bottom')
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

Australia: data ever for 17/21 indicators; only 13/21 have any reading after 2020.

7. Critical assessment¶

  • The conclusion is stable; the exact rank is not. The Monte-Carlo test is explicit: above the OECD median in 89% of runs, top third in 44%. We report the range rather than a single over-precise number.
  • Relative is not the same as absolute. Z-scores measure Australia against a moving group, so Finding 3 uses within-country trends and Finding 5 an external, absolute scale. Three different lenses point to the same conclusion about the subjective decline, and that agreement is what gives it weight.
  • Compulsory voting. Australia's biggest strength on the composite comes from a legal requirement to vote, not from a spontaneous sign of civic health. Letting it lift the rank by eight places would be misleading, so we measure its effect rather than leave it buried.
  • Correlation, not causation. We show that Australia is a negative outlier on loneliness and deaths of despair, but we do not claim to know why. Housing costs, migration patterns, access to services and changes in measurement are all plausible, and none can be tested with this data. The finding says where to look, not why.
  • Survivorship in the data. Because the subjective and relational series are the ones that disappear, the real decline is more likely understated than overstated here.
  • External data, used narrowly. We add exactly one external series, to fill one identified gap and measure the one quantity that went missing, rather than to pad the argument.

8. Wider context¶

In July 2023 the Australian Treasury released Measuring What Matters, the country's first national well-being framework, with five themes, twelve dimensions and fifty indicators, designed to track progress "beyond economic measures" (the dashboard moved to the ABS in 2024). In effect the government has already accepted the premise of this report: that GDP and a single headline number are not enough, and that health, security, sustainability and social cohesion have to be read together.

Our results line up with that framework and with the politics of the mid-2020s. Australia's material strengths are the familiar "lucky country" ones: resources, a resilient labour market and long lives. The decline we isolate, a rise in loneliness and deaths of despair that outpaces most of the OECD, a record-low happiness score and persistent housing stress, is the same ground as the country's cost-of-living and mental-health debate. The data do more than answer "is Australia doing well?"; they point to where attention should go: less to income, which is in good shape, and more to social connection and distress.

9. Conclusion¶

Is Australia doing well? It is above average, and quietly sliding on the things that are hardest to measure.

The tests agree on the shape of the answer. On the material measures (income, jobs, longevity) Australia is a durable and still-improving success. On the social and subjective measures (social connection, mental distress, life satisfaction) it is going backwards, faster than most of the OECD, and the data itself is losing sight of exactly those dimensions. A single composite scores Australia a comfortable 14th and, in doing so, hides the trend that matters most for what the country does next.

For a deliberately vague question, the honest answer is not one confident number. It is a breakdown, stress-tested, that shows where to look. On this data, that means Australia's weakening social and mental-health indicators, not the material measures it already does well on.

Reproducibility: place OECD Data.csv beside this notebook and Run All (pandas, numpy, matplotlib). Sources: OECD How's Life? Well-being Database (2010–2025); World Happiness Report / Gallup World Poll (life evaluation, used to fill the post-2020 subjective well-being gap).
